exemplar.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Consistent, sustained focus on the prescribed title and clear links to Mathematics and Human Sciences (Criterion A)
Use of concrete, relevant examples (Pythagoras, normal distribution, supply-and-demand, Keynesian models) that ground the discussion (Criterion C)
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive, with limited unpacking of underlying knowledge questions such as idealization and reliability (Criterion B)
Structural and coherence issues: incomplete transitions, abrupt paragraph endings, and a conclusion that summarizes rather than reflects on TOK implications (Criterion D)

annotations.comment: Discussion of the supply and demand model describes its usefulness but lacks critical evaluation of its key assumptions (e.g., rational actors, perfect information) and their effect on predictive accuracy.
Referring to the normal distribution as a âTheoremâ is inaccurate; use precise terminologyâthis is a statistical distribution, not a theoremâto maintain disciplinary clarity.
Boxâs claim is restated without analysis of its implications for mathematical modelling; deepen engagement by examining how idealisation shapes both the power and the limitations of these models.
The personal reflection on using the Pythagorean theorem in the studentâs internal assessment adds a valuable autobiographical perspective and shows relevance to lived experience.
Breretonâs historical context for the normal distribution is cited but not fully integrated into the argument; explain how its development informs our understanding of model reliability over time.
Online sources are listed without descriptive titles or consistent access dates; ensure each URL entry includes a clear title, date accessed, and conforms to the chosen citation format.
The citation for Gupta (2021) omits the journal name and DOI, hindering source verification; verify that all references are complete and traceable.
The claim that models provide good approximations without capturing every detail is promising but underdeveloped; expand this insight to explore the trade-offs inherent in model construction.

Referring broadly to âHuman Sciencesâ without justification is too general; since the essay focuses on economics, the student should clarify why this discipline exemplifies the tension between simplicity and accuracy.

The introduction defines models generally but fails to link this definition to the TOK knowledge questions of idealisation and reliability; integrating these concepts would better set up the essayâs analytical focus.

The student explicitly states the prescribed title and chosen areas of knowledge but does not articulate a guiding knowledge question to frame the analysis; adding one would sharpen the focus and connect examples to TOK concepts more effectively.
The paragraph ends abruptly with âModels,â indicating an incomplete thought and disrupting coherence; revise to complete the argument or transition clearly to the next point.
The COVID-19 panic-buying example aptly illustrates irrational behavior but needs explicit linkage back to the knowledge question on predictability and the limits of model assumptions.

Presenting Friedmanâs view as contradictory to Boxâs claim is misleading; discuss how predictive performance and model âusefulnessâ can actually complement Boxâs insight into model wrongness.
Stating that models were ânever made to be usefulâ misrepresents their epistemic purpose; refine this to reflect that usefulness arises from successful prediction despite known flaws.
The conclusion summarizes prior examples but does not address the broader implications of accepting Boxâs claim for the production and evaluation of knowledge in TOK terms.
Citation of â(Oxford)â lacks a full reference in the bibliography; ensure each source cited in text has complete details in the works cited to maintain academic rigor.
Listing the normal distribution and Pythagorean theorem remains descriptive; unpack how their simplifying assumptions concretely illustrate Boxâs tension between simplicity and accuracy.
Quotations from Berlinski and Taleb are valuable but are only used descriptively; critically compare their perspectives to evaluate the claim from multiple viewpoints.
The sentence trailing âsubsides anâ is truncated and leaves the reader hanging; ensure transitions are complete to maintain a coherent line of reasoning.
The studentâs real-world housing market example from their own economics commentary adds authenticity and shows self-awareness of model limitations in practice.
Acknowledging multicultural factors in the housing market is insightful but the student does not analyze how such cultural nuance concretely undermines the supply-and-demand modelâs predictions.
The reference to âOuliarisâ lacks a publication date and context; include full bibliographic details and explain how its methodology discussion informs the evaluation of economic models.
Closure relies on impact statements rather than engaging with TOK concepts like certainty, bias, and the evolving nature of knowledge; consider framing the final reflection around these ideas.
Works cited entries lack uniform formatting and essential details (publisher, edition, date); standardize citation style and include full information for each source.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
